name: The Name Game (Pawgress Edition)
instructions: Say the dog's name once. The moment they look at you, mark with a 'Yes!' or a click, and deliver a high-value treat. Repeat 10-15 times per session in low-distraction environments.
command_human: Say the dog's name in an upbeat tone (e.g., 'Fido!').
command_dog: Immediate eye contact with the handler.
reward: High-value treat + enthusiastic verbal praise.
key_attributes: STRICT RULE: Do not repeat the name if they ignore you. This prevents 'Name Burn'. If they are too distracted, do not call them. Instead, use a lure or kissing noise to get attention.

name: Leash Pressure Yielding
instructions: Apply slow, steady pressure on the leash sideways or backwards. Do not pull the dog, just hold tension. The exact second the dog steps into the pressure to create slack, mark 'Yes!' and reward.
command_human: No verbal command. Purely physical leash pressure.
command_dog: Step into the direction of the leash pull to create slack.
reward: Treat delivered close to your leg, reinforcing closeness.
key_attributes: Never jerk the leash. It must be slow, escalating tension until they yield.

name: The Place Command
instructions: Lure the dog onto a raised bed or specific mat. Say 'Place'. Reward for all four paws on. Build duration slowly. If they break early, calmly redirect them back without a reward.
command_dog: All four paws on the bed, holding position until released.
environment: Living room or visible household area.
reward: Treat delivered strictly ON the bed.
key_attributes: Duration is more important than distance at first. Reward them periodically for just staying there.

name: Sit & Stay
instructions: Command 'Sit'. Introduce the 'Stay' hand signal (flat hand towards face). Take one step back. If they hold, return and reward. Slowly increase the distance and duration.
command_human: 'Sit' followed by 'Stay' with a stop-sign hand signal.
command_dog: Maintain the sit position despite handler movement.
reward: Treat and calm praise (do not over-excite).
key_attributes: Master the 'Three Ds': Distance, Duration, Distraction. Increase only one at a time.

name: Down & Stay
instructions: From a sit, lure the dog down to the floor. Command 'Down'. Introduce 'Stay'. The Down is a naturally more relaxed state, aim for longer durations here while you walk around the room.
command_human: 'Down' with a point to the floor, then 'Stay'.
command_dog: Lie completely flat, elbows touching the ground.
environment: Inside the house with mild family distractions.
reward: Treat placed between their front paws.
key_attributes: If they pop up, immediately reset them without saying a word. Consistency is key.

name: Fetch & Retrieve
instructions: Throw a low-value toy a short distance. When the dog picks it up, enthusiastically call them back. Show a higher-value toy or treat to encourage them to run to you and drop the first object.
command_human: 'Fetch' on throw, 'Here' or dog's name for return.
command_dog: Pick up item, return to handler immediately.
environment: Backyard or open field on a long line.
reward: Tug-of-war with a second toy or a high-value treat.
key_attributes: Never chase a dog that won't bring it back. Run away from them to trigger their chase instinct.

name: Drop It (Trade Game)
instructions: While the dog has a toy, present a high-value treat right at their nose. As they open their mouth to take the treat, say 'Drop It'. Let them eat the treat, and give the toy back to build trust.
command_human: 'Drop It' exactly as they open their mouth.
command_dog: Release object willingly.
reward: High-value treat PLUS returning the toy.
key_attributes: The dog needs to learn that 'drop it' doesn't mean playtime is over; it means they get paid, and playtime continues.

name: Nosework: Search
instructions: Put the dog in a stay or have someone hold them. Hide a smelly treat or favorite toy in a different room. Release with 'Search!' Start easy (visible) and progressively make the hiding spots harder.
command_human: 'Search!' or 'Find It!' in an excited tone.
command_dog: Use nose to locate hidden object.
environment: Around the house, moving to different rooms.
reward: The hidden treat itself, or a massive tug-party when they find the toy.
key_attributes: Mental exhaustion is often better than physical exhaustion. This will tire your dog out fast.

name: Confidence: Tactile Walking
instructions: Lure the dog to walk over strange, safe surfaces (wobbly boards, bubble wrap, grates, low tables). Reward heavily for interacting with the scary object. Do not force them.
command_dog: Engage bravely with a new, weird surface.
environment: Garage, park benches, hardware store floors.
reward: Continuous stream of small treats as they conquer the obstacle.
key_attributes: Never drag them. Let them investigate on their own terms. Confidence is built through self-discovery.

name: Agility: Basic Jump
instructions: Set a jump bar very low (or use a broomstick on books). With the dog on leash, jog toward it and say 'Hup' or 'Jump' as you both go over it. Reward heavily on the landing side.
command_dog: Clear the obstacle without knocking it down.
environment: Backyard or grass field. Soft landings only.
reward: Thrown toy or treat to encourage forward momentum.
key_attributes: Keep jumps low to protect their developing joints, especially in dogs under 18 months.

name: Structured Heel
instructions: Hold leash short. Command 'Heel'. The dog's shoulder should align with your leg. If they drift ahead, stop immediately and walk backward, patting your leg until they return to position.
command_human: 'Heel' when stepping off on your left foot.
command_dog: Walk in strict parallel with the handler, shoulder at handler's seam.
reward: Treat delivered directly down at the seam of your pants.
key_attributes: Heeling is active work. Do not let them sniff or pee while under the Heel command. Give them a separate 'Break' or 'Free' command for casual walking.

name: Threshold Boundaries
instructions: Walk to an exterior door. Command 'Sit'. Slowly open the door. If the dog breaks the sit to rush out, immediately close the door. Repeat until the door is wide open and the dog remains seated.
command_dog: Wait patiently at the open door until explicitly invited out.
environment: Front door or car crate door.
reward: The reward is the permission to go outside ('Free!').
key_attributes: This is a life-saving drill preventing dogs from darting into traffic. Be incredibly strict.

name: Leave It
instructions: Place a low-value treat on the floor and cover it with your hand. The dog will sniff/paw. Wait. The moment they pull away and look at you, say 'Yes!', pick it up, and give them a *different*, better treat from your other hand.
command_human: 'Leave It' in a firm, calm tone.
command_dog: Disengage completely from the object of interest.
reward: A totally separate, much better treat.
key_attributes: Never reward them with the object they were told to leave. It teaches them that 'Leave It' just means 'Wait'.

name: House Training: Scheduled Intervals
instructions: Take the dog outside to the exact same spot immediately after waking, eating, playing, and every 1-2 hours. Stand still and wait 5 minutes. The *second* they eliminate, praise enthusiastically and give a high-value treat.
command_human: 'Go Potty' when outside in the grass.
command_dog: Eliminate in the designated outdoor area.
environment: A specific, boring patch of grass in the yard.
reward: Massive treat party directly after finishing (not inside).
key_attributes: Do not play with them until the bathroom transaction is complete. Business first, fun second.

name: House Training: The Food Machine Crate
instructions: Transform the crate into a 'Food Machine'. Toss high-value treats into the crate. When the dog enters, mark 'Yes!' and reward again. Practice closing the door for only 5-10 seconds while feeding through the grate.
command_dog: Enter crate willingly and settle down quietly.
environment: Bedroom or quiet corner of the house.
reward: Continuous stream of treats for staying inside.
key_attributes: Build a positive association before ever using it for overnight stays. Never use it as a place of punishment.

name: House Training: The Bell Game
instructions: Hang bells on the door. Every single time you take the dog out for a potty break, take their paw or nose and ring the bell, say 'Outside', and open the door. Soon they will ring it themselves to request a break.
command_dog: Nudge the bell with paw/nose when needing to eliminate.
reward: The door opens immediately.
key_attributes: If they start ringing the bell just to play outside, put them on a leash, take them to pee, and go back inside immediately. It is for business only.

name: Puppy: Bite Inhibition
instructions: When the puppy bites your hands/clothes during play, make a high-pitched 'Yip!' sound and immediately stop all play and ignore them for 10 seconds. Resume play with an appropriate chew toy.
command_dog: Release pressure immediately upon hearing the sound.
reward: Resuming play with an allowed toy.
key_attributes: Consistency among all family members is required. The puppy must learn human skin is extremely fragile.

name: Hunting: Scent Tracking Intro
instructions: Drag a high-value scent (like a hotdog or specific training scent) through short grass in a straight line for 10 feet. Bring the dog to the start line and say 'Find It'. Reward heavily at the end of the track.
command_human: 'Find It' or 'Search' at the start of the trail.
command_dog: Keep nose to the ground, following the exact trail.
reward: A pile of treats at the very end of the trail.
key_attributes: Walk the trail with the wind at your back so the scent blows forward, forcing them to use the ground track instead of air scenting.

name: Hunting: Steadiness
instructions: Command 'Sit/Stay' next to you. Throw a bumper or toy out into the yard. The dog must NOT break the hold to chase it. They must wait for your specific release command (e.g. 'Back') before retrieving.
command_dog: Mark the fall of the object visually without breaking the sit.
reward: Getting to retrieve the bumper is the primary reward.
key_attributes: If they break early to chase it, intercept them or have a helper pick it up before they can get it. Breaking breaks the game.

name: Hunting: Soft-Mouth Retrieve
instructions: Wrap a hard bumper in slightly uncomfortable material (like tough wire mesh or zip ties, cut safe) to discourage hard biting. Praise the dog for gently carrying the object back to the hand without chomping.
command_dog: Carry object with a soft grip, no teeth puncturing.
reward: Praise and a treat upon successful gentle delivery to hand.
key_attributes: Do not play tug-of-war with a hunting dog. It encourages hard biting and thrashing.

name: Hunting: Gunfire Desensitization
instructions: While the dog is highly engaged in positive play or eating from far away (50+ yards), have a helper make a simulated popping noise (like a clap or starter pistol). If the dog ignores it, reward heavily. Slowly move closer over weeks.
command_dog: Ignore loud noises and remain focused on the task/food.
reward: Continuous feed or high-drive play during the noise.
key_attributes: Never shoot right over a fresh dog. Start incredibly far away and watch for signs of fear. Move closer only when they don't even flinch.

name: Hunting: Blind Retrieve Line Drill
instructions: Place a bumper in a known location while the dog cannot see. Line the dog up facing the exact direction. Use your hand to cast a straight line over their head and command 'Back'. Stop them with a whistle if they veer off course.
command_dog: Run straight on a frozen line, trusting the handler completely.
environment: Long, clean field like a soccer pitch.
reward: Retrieval of the hidden bumper.
key_attributes: This requires massive trust. Start very short (10 yards) with highly visible bumpers, eventually moving to long distances where the dog must trust you blindly.

name: Hallway Restraint Recall
instructions: Requires 2 people. Have one person hold the dog in a narrow hallway. The handler moves 15-20 feet away. Handler calls 'Here!' or name in an excited tone. Helper releases as dog tries to lunge toward handler. Mark 'Yes!' on arrival.
command_dog: Fast, motivated run to the handler.
reward: Massive praise + High-value treat + Tug play.
key_attributes: Builds high motivation for the recall before adding distractions.

name: Positive Collar Desensitization
instructions: Feed the dog treats with one hand. With the other hand, firmly but gently grip their collar. Move the collar slightly. Mark 'Yes!' while still holding it and deliver another treat. Release the collar after the treat.
command_dog: Accept physical restraint calmly without nipping.
reward: Treats while being touched/held.
key_attributes: Prevents 'Collar Shy' behavior and hand-nipping during handling.

name: House Line Management
instructions: Let the dog drag a light, handle-less leash indoors. If they chew it, wiggle the line gently to disrupt them. The moment they stop and look at you, mark 'Yes!' and reward. Use it to prevent jumping or wandering.
command_dog: Ignoring the line and focusing on the handler.
reward: Attaining access to play or treats for calm focus.
key_attributes: Gives you a 6-foot reach to redirect behavior without confronting the dog directly.

name: Advanced Trade (Out Command)
instructions: Wiggle a toy like prey to build drive. When the dog has it, offer a treat directly to their nose. Say 'Out' precisely as they drop the toy for the treat. Let them eat, then immediately offer the toy back to play.
command_dog: Release toy/object willingly for a reward.
reward: Reward plus the continuation of play.
key_attributes: Teaches the dog that 'Out' doesn't mean play ends, it means they get paid and keep playing.
